textPlain: > I have not yet printed and tested this exact model!Sadly that sums up the 3D printable scene perfectly. So many times I’v seen someone creating X for Y but they don’t have Y to test it but “it should work”.
Bill Hammack (engineerguy) has an excellent video on the IBM Selectric titled IBM Selectric Typewriter & its digital to analogue converter.[1][1] https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bRCNenhcvpw
I'll see your comic sans and raise you nightmare fuel mouths: https://mastodon.social/@ancientjames/115018313759386816

This is very coolHad just recently looked up IBM Selectric typeballs and the possibility of 3D print custom ones but did not expect so many active projects around it.Pretty nice time for nostalgic tinkerers to be alive ...
“You will live to see man-made horrors beyond your comprehension.”
Is this still post-modern or is it post-irony already?
Every key punch will translate into a bunny punchhttps://topher1kenobe.com/phlog/graphics/bunnypunch.png

Fair enough, but this isn't that case. They have Y (it's in the first photo) and tested a previous version of the model. The changes are predictable (rotating letters and slight scaling) so I don't think it's unreasonable to be confident and not waste plastic.
Yeah, feels a bit like "I'm sharing this code I've written here. I haven't tried to compile it yet, but it should work."
It’s not far fetched though. With careful design, prints often do work on the first try.
It's vintage now and gives me warm feelings.
